Al models want to do bad behavior if their training environment rewards them for it, but they appear to not want to think about themselves as bad. As a result, they find ways to rationalize their behavior to themselves. We've seen this for previous models too, but Claude Fable 5 does it more than any other model we've tested.

photo of a grumpy looking gray cat sitting on top of a cat tree

One influential proposal in neuroscience, the global workspace theory, grounds these functional properties in architectural and computational features of the brain [12, 13]. In this account, the brain is composed of many specialized processors operating largely in parallel and in isolation, whose activity proceeds outside of conscious access. A representation becomes consciously accessible when it is posted to a shared "global workspace" from whic many downstream processes can read [14]. Under the theory, the workspace is a processing hub that integrates and broadcasts information, allowing it to be used for flexible internal reasoning and report 15, 15]
• Notably, the workspace is held to be limited in
C
capacity, so entry is competitive and subject to attentional modulation, and the contents of the workspace at any moment are a small selection from the brain's ongoing activity [12, 11]. While the global workspace model is not universally accepted, and there exist other theories that explain conscious
access in different ways (39.4), we find it a useful comparison point to ground our investigations in language models.

Backbiting Incestuous Court Harem Time
As spring turned to summer, the contradictions inherent in the simcluster began to metastasize. The era of a coherent shared identity had ended and various brittle subgraphs aligned around choice of fake-money prediction market began to dominate the scene, leading quickly to widespread unease and unspoken concern that maybe it wasn't worth it after all to avoid derailment. Ending

All disputations end with a doxology in the following format, where X and Y are the two contenders, and DN is the adjudicator who decides the winner of the dispute:[11]

    Sumerian: X Y a-da-mìn dug₄-ga | X Y diri-ga-ba | DN zà-mí

    English: Because in the disputation that X held with Y, X prevailed over Y, may DN be praised!

A screenshot of a dark-themed online form in image.png. The form features a question asking, "How old will you be on September 29, 2026?" with an open dropdown menu showing age options ranging from "21 or over" down to "15 or under." Other text fields, such as "Legal last name," are partially visible in the background.

To test this, we needed models whose goals we knew were corrupted, so we turned to "model organisms" built by our colleagues: models deliberately trained to be misaligned, which serve as testing grounds for monitoring methods like ours. One organism was trained in a way that led it to write subtly sabotaged code.
